Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'm 27 and working part-time while juggling 17 online courses, so I had to squeeze studying in whenever I could. I'd usually listen to lecture recordings during my commute or while doing chores since I learn best by hearing things. I tried to dedicate my evenings to actual studying, maybe three hours a night when I could manage it, but life kept getting in the way. Some weeks I'd hit my 23 hours, other weeks I'd barely manage half that.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my personal life. Deadlines definitely drove my schedule more than any planned routine. When multiple assignments piled up, I'd have these intense study marathons, then I'd crash and barely touch anything for days. I wished I could be more consistent, but between work and everything else, regular times just weren't realistic for me. The stress of constantly adjusting was exhausting.
Q3: Usually it was the anxiety of knowing something was due or the fear of missing an announcement. I'd get that nagging feeling that I was falling behind, which happened a lot with my attendance being so spotty. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any new discussion posts from classmates since the peer support really helped me feel less alone. Other times it was pure panic when I realized a deadline was closer than I thought.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became part of my morning coffee routine, like a habit I developed to stay on top of things. I'd log in first thing, skim through announcements, maybe listen to part of a video while getting ready. But that routine fell apart during really stressful periods when I just couldn't face seeing all the things I hadn't done. When peer support sessions were happening regularly, that actually pulled me back in more consistently because I didn't want to let my study partners down.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline since I'm an auditory learner and I watched over 270 minutes of them. Being able to replay explanations until they clicked was invaluable. The discussion forums were also really helpful because connecting with other students made me feel less isolated in my struggles. My peer support group specifically helped me understand concepts I was missing from the lectures I couldn't attend in person.
Q6: I tended to ignore the reading materials and PDFs because sitting and reading just doesn't work for my brain the same way. If something wasn't in audio or video format, I'd often put it off indefinitely. Some of the supplementary resources felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. I know my assignment completion rate shows this—only about a third done—and a lot of that was because I avoided the text-heavy work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to passing the course or when it helped me actually understand something that had been confusing me. If I could listen to it while doing something else, even better. The peer discussions felt worthwhile because they weren't just busywork—they were real conversations with people who got what I was going through. Anything that felt like just checking a box, I struggled to prioritize.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're barely keeping your head above water with the required stuff, optional feels like a cruel joke. Required activities had this weight of consequence that forced me to engage even when I didn't want to. But sometimes the optional peer discussions were more valuable than required readings because they actually helped me learn, so the distinction felt arbitrary.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it multiple times on my phone during work breaks or while exercising. I had a notebook full of handwritten notes from those listening sessions. I also used some external YouTube videos and podcasts on similar topics when I needed concepts explained differently. My actual learning time was way more than what the system could track.
Q10: There were whole weeks where I was actively studying but barely touched the LMS. I'd be reviewing my downloaded materials, discussing concepts with my peer support group over text messages, or re-listening to recordings I'd saved. Sometimes I'd access a video once, download the audio, and then study from that for days without logging back in. The LMS would show me as inactive when I was actually working really hard.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online, then download it or find ways to access it offline for repeated listening. With my spotty attendance, I relied heavily on being able to study materials on my own time without needing internet. Some lecture recordings I must have listened to five or six times, but the LMS only knows about that first view. It was the only way I could make the auditory learning work for my schedule.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when the workload peaked and my motivation was already struggling. I felt this heavy sadness that made everything harder—getting out of bed, facing my responsibilities, believing I could actually pass. My stress was constant, and some days the anxiety made it impossible to even open the LMS without feeling overwhelmed. The peer support helped pull me through those dark patches, but it was a real fight.
Q13: All of those things hit me at different points. Fatigue was huge because I was working and studying and not sleeping enough. When I was confused about material, I'd avoid the LMS because seeing my incomplete tasks just made me feel worse about myself. Work responsibilities sometimes meant I had to choose between a paycheck and a deadline, and the paycheck usually won. My LMS usage would drop dramatically during those overwhelming periods.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd log in, start watching a video, and realize twenty minutes later that I hadn't absorbed a single word because my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. When the sadness was heavy, I could be staring at the screen but completely checked out mentally. The engagement looked real on paper but wasn't always meaningful.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was the moderate anxiety making me avoid things that felt too hard or too likely to show my failures. Part of it was just being exhausted and telling myself I'd do it tomorrow. The written assignments especially got pushed back because they required a type of focus I couldn't always summon. I knew my assignment completion rate was suffering but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my avoidance patterns. I'd have these bursts of frantic activity right before something was due, logging in multiple times, trying to cram everything in. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and procrastinate. The LMS probably shows these spikes of activity followed by quiet periods. It wasn't healthy, but that external pressure was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my low motivation.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it could go either way. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days because I felt like a failure. With my moderate resilience, I could eventually bounce back, but it took time. I wished feedback came faster sometimes because waiting made my anxiety worse.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming with 17 courses to manage. Having so much content spread across different modules made it hard to know where to focus. The flexibility was both a blessing and a curse—it let me work around my schedule but also let me fall behind easily. I gravitated toward the parts that had clear deadlines and peer interaction because those gave me structure I couldn't create myself.
Q19: The video content being available to download or re-watch made engagement much easier for someone like me who needs to hear things multiple times. The discussion forums and peer support features were genuinely helpful for staying connected. But the sheer volume of courses and the text-heavy materials made engagement harder. When attendance isn't mandatory but material builds on itself, missing classes creates this snowball effect that's hard to recover from.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well sometimes. You can be logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions, really trying to understand the material—but then the actual assignments feel like a different beast. For me, understanding something and being able to produce work demonstrating that understanding are two different skills. The anxiety around submitting imperfect work can keep you stuck in learning mode without ever crossing into completion mode.
Q21: That could definitely be someone like me on my better weeks. When I was studying effectively offline—listening to downloaded lectures, working with my peer group outside the LMS—my activity looked low but my learning was happening. My exam score of 68 wasn't amazing but it showed I understood more than my 32% assignment completion would suggest. Sometimes the most effective studying happens away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how long you watch videos, whether you participate in discussions. That's useful data. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the emotional state behind the activity. They can't tell if you're actively learning or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture the peer support conversations happening over text or the repeated listening to downloaded materials.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when you genuinely want to discuss ideas with peers. For me, engagement also means showing up emotionally even when it's hard—pushing through the sadness and anxiety to stay connected to my education. It's not just about logging hours; it's about being mentally present.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It can't capture the tears of frustration or the pride when I finally understood something difficult. It doesn't know about my peer support group's late-night text conversations that kept me going. It can't measure resilience or the emotional labor of being a 27-year-old trying to build a better future while barely holding things together. The data shows my struggles but not my strength.
